question: Why are you interested in joining Tailscale? Thoughtful responses are encouraged.*
answer:
What draws me to Tailscale is the shape of the BizOps & Strategy mandate: being the embedded operator for the cross-functional problems that don't have a natural owner, and owning them from diagnosis through a durable, handed-off solution. That is exactly how I have worked. At M Science I led a cross-functional overhaul of our packaging and pricing model, aligning data science, sales, and research, standing up a pricing desk, and driving a ~35% ARR increase the quarter after launch, then built the infrastructure to make it stick: a Salesforce master-data system that unified our product metadata and automated entitlements, serving as the semantic layer behind our analytics and customer portal. I like building those structures early so they can be handed off as a company scales.

The specific problems in this role map directly to my experience: pricing and packaging strategy, maturing GTM systems alongside RevOps, reducing rework in business systems, and doing my own data analysis in SQL and Python rather than waiting on someone else. I am also genuinely excited by a PLG, developer-facing company: earning adoption bottom-up rewards exactly the kind of quantitative, first-principles operator I try to be. The written, async culture and high tolerance for ambiguity suit how I work best. I do my sharpest thinking when I get to define the problem, not just answer a scoped one. Tailscale's next chapter, from international/APAC expansion to cross-company AI adoption, is full of the ambiguous, high-leverage problems I want to spend my time on.

Describe a product you have managed or helped develop, ideally one with data-intensive or internal platform components. What was the core user problem, how did you identify it, and how did you work with your team to solve it?
answer: In my current role I manage an API product that presents our investor clients with forecasts for critical KPIs across hundreds of companies. Through product discovery I identified two issues blocking adoption by quant investors: the API's structure caused data restatements that created look-ahead bias and invalidated their models, and clients lacked the semantic context to appropriately leverage the data. To address both, I collaborated with engineering to design a new API schema - introducing a point-in-time history methodology for restatements, working with our investment research team to create standardized dimension tables for company and metric metadata, and adding associations to standard industry datasets via external IDs. Ahead of the launch I worked with sales and marketing leadership to craft a GTM strategy for the updated product, which drove a steep increase in new-business deals.

question: What is the hardest you have ever worked on something? What was it, and why?
answer: The hardest I've ever worked was on a project to build a new entitlements system, where I was responsible for taking a manual process that took 40+ hours a week and building an automated process to power our customer entitlements. Though the work would save the company countless hours, building the system required a tremendous investment of manual validation and transformation to populate the data. To accomplish it, I first sought ways to cut down the scope - reaching out to data science and engineering colleagues to investigate automating or optimizing, then personally writing a Python script to do complex mapping and reduce the number of records to review. Once every avenue had been exhausted or implemented, I led a team of 3 through two weeks of dedicated manual validation, personally reviewing and correcting over 10,000 records. I believe operations professionals are most successful when they learn to identify which work can be simplified and automated and which requires doing difficult, undesirable work by hand - and, having determined this work was necessary to do manually, I did it.

question: Why are you interested in working at Hive? (strategic problem solving)
answer: As someone who has built my career on seeking out and solving big problems, I'm drawn to products that solve a real, tangible problem for their users. That's the point of any product - but where in my current role I'm solving a problem for institutional investors by optimizing a data product to squeeze the most they can out of trades, Hive directly addresses a real-world need for its target user. I'm looking for the opportunity to leverage what I've learned solving strategic problems in high-growth, highly technical environments to improve a product I can feel passionate about, and I'm confident Hive fits that bill.

question: Please share what you did day-to-day and what you created/built.
answer: As a Product Strategy Associate at M Science I initiated, led, and executed high-value strategic projects and overhauled internal processes and systems. The day-to-day varied - my role was to be the ultimate generalist: breaking strategic goals into actionable, executable steps and delivering on them with whatever tools, resources, and collaborators I could muster while aligning stakeholders. One example was building a master-data system for product metadata. I had identified that a major source of internal friction was that the organization lacked a clear, shared definition of what our 'product' even was - different teams bucketed our output differently, which led to siloed work, confusing processes, and no ability to properly sell our work as M Science moved away from enterprise deals. I worked with key stakeholders to create a single source of truth on product definitions and our explicit offerings, and to associate our disparate systems into one interoperable platform - work then leveraged across key business systems including our customer portal, the semantic layer of our AI product, product entitlements, unit economics, and internal analytics, all of which I either built directly or built closely with stakeholders.

question: What Excites You About This Role?
answer: What excites me most is the chance to turn emerging AI capabilities into agent experiences that compensation teams trust enough to make real pay decisions with. I've spent my career running customer discovery on technically sophisticated, data-intensive products, then partnering closely with engineering to ship what solves the root problem rather than the requested feature. At M Science I did exactly that with our KPI API: product discovery surfaced adoption blockers that quant teams hadn't framed as product problems, and the new schema we built with engineering doubled the number of quant teams using the data and grew ARR by ~80%. I'm also a builder by instinct. At Audare AI I used prototyping tools like Bolt and Lovable to put concepts in front of users early instead of debating them in a doc. Compa's agent-first bet on a function as high-stakes as compensation is exactly the kind of problem I want to work on.

question: Cover letter
answer:
I want to join Linear because it is one of the few companies that treats the tools software teams use every day as a craft worth obsessing over, and it shows in a product people genuinely love to use. I have spent my career building the systems and tools that let teams do better work. At M Science I conceptualized and built an internal master-data platform in Salesforce that unified our product metadata and became the source of truth behind our customer portal, entitlements, unit economics, and analytics, because I could see how much siloed, duplicated work was slowing everyone down. I care about that kind of leverage, where getting the underlying model right quietly makes an entire organization faster.

I am a generalist operator at heart. I pull insight from noise, turn it into a coherent strategy, and then lead the execution myself, whether that means running product discovery with customers, designing a new API schema with engineering, or writing the Python to clean 10,000 records so a system can finally launch. On my current KPI API product that approach doubled the number of quant teams using our data and grew ARR by roughly 80%.

Linear builds for exactly the kind of user I want to serve: people who care about their craft and want tools that respect it. I would love to bring my mix of product judgment, technical fluency, and bias for execution to that mission.

question: What motivates you professionally? What do you look forward to about work every day?
answer:
What motivates me is curiosity and the satisfaction of turning something messy into something that works. I have a compulsive need to understand how a system actually functions, and I like sitting at the intersection of data, strategy, and execution: taking a chaotic set of inputs, finding the real problem underneath, and shipping something that measurably moves the business.

What I look forward to day to day is the problem-solving itself. I enjoy breaking an ambiguous problem down to first principles and building a novel, sometimes unconventional, path to a solution, then staying hands-on in the execution rather than handing it off. I am happiest when I am close enough to the details to write the query, design the schema, or talk to the user directly. The best days are the ones where a hard problem finally clicks and the thing I built makes other people's work easier.

question: Who are your biggest professional influences? What specific things have you taken from them?
answer:
Two kinds of influences have shaped how I work. The first is intellectual: Isaac Asimov's idea of psychohistory in Foundation, the notion that you can find patterns in data and turn chaotic human systems into something interpretable, is a big part of why I gravitated toward economics and then to data and product. It taught me to look for the underlying structure in messy problems, which is still how I approach almost everything I build.

The second is the leaders early in my career who trusted me with real problems before I had the obvious credentials for them, handing a junior employee ownership of a company-wide pricing overhaul. What I took from them was a bias toward giving people responsibility and judgment rather than just tasks, and the confidence to operate as a generalist: to get to the root of a problem, form a defensible point of view, and own the execution end to end. I try to bring both the structured thinking and that sense of ownership to every team I join.

question: What is your highest level of education?

The role requires deep hands-on experience building with AI. Describe a project where you leveraged core LLM concepts (e.g., prompting, fine-tuning, embeddings, retrieval) and translate them into a reliable, user-facing product. What was the greatest technical challenge you faced, and how did you resolve it?
answer: At M Science I built the AI-driven data-QA layer for our KPI API product, which forecasts financial KPIs for hundreds of companies for quant investors. The user-facing requirement was near-perfect accuracy, since clients trade on this data. I used an OCR/LLM pipeline to read and structure messy source financial documents into standardized metric values, prompting the model to extract and normalize fields, paired with an ML anomaly-detection layer that scored incoming values against historical patterns. The greatest technical challenge was reliability: LLM extraction on inconsistent, noisy filings produced silent errors that were unacceptable for a product clients depend on. I resolved it by not trusting the model in isolation. I built the anomaly detector as a second, independent check to catch outliers the extraction missed, and added a human-in-the-loop review gate for flagged exceptions, so the automated path handled the high-confidence bulk and people adjudicated the edges. That combination cut the product's annual error rate from over 2% to roughly zero.

question: Walk us through a time you "vibe-coded a prototype before lunch and wrote the rollout plan after." Describe a messy operational problem you turned into an elegant automated system using AI, detailing the full journey from prototype to scaled production.
answer: One of the messiest operational problems I owned was customer entitlements at M Science, which ran on a manual process that consumed more than 40 hours a week and was error-prone. I started by prototyping the automation myself: I wrote a Python mapping script to model how our disparate product metadata should resolve into entitlements, which let me quickly test the logic and cut the number of records that needed manual review. Once the approach held up, I built it into an internal Salesforce master-data system that became the single source of truth for product metadata, serving as the semantic layer for our AI products and automating entitlements going forward. Getting to production still required real operational work: I led a three-person team over two weeks to validate and correct more than 10,000 records the automation couldn't reach, because I judged that this was the necessary manual work to make the automated system trustworthy. The result replaced a recurring 40-plus hour weekly manual process with an automated one, and the same master-data layer went on to power our customer portal, unit economics, and internal analytics.

question: Tell us a little bit about yourself and why you're a fit for this position.

I am a generalist operator who has built my career on pulling signal out of noise, turning it into strategy, and then doing the operational work to execute it. At M Science I own product strategy and operations for a data API used by quantitative investors. question: Are you based in the U.S?
What excites you about Replit?
answer:
What excites me about Replit is that it collapses the distance between an idea and a working product. I have spent my career as the generalist who takes a problem from first principles all the way to a shipped solution, and Replit is building the platform that lets far more people do exactly that. In my own work I have leaned heavily on AI to move faster: I built an automated data-QA layer for our KPI API using an OCR and LLM pipeline plus anomaly detection that cut the product's annual data error rate from over 2% to near zero. Experiences like that convinced me that agentic, AI-native tooling is going to reshape who gets to build software, and Replit is at the center of that shift.

I am also drawn to the product surface itself. Replit has to serve everyone from first-time builders to professional engineers, which makes segmentation, onboarding, and monetization genuinely hard problems, the kind I have spent years solving. At M Science I ran discovery on a technically sophisticated product, rebuilt it around how power users actually worked, and grew ARR by roughly 80%. I would love to bring that combination of product discovery, technical fluency, and execution to a product I would genuinely use myself.

question: What is your desired salary range?
What makes you excited about the ElevenLabs mission?
answer: ElevenLabs is making voice a first-class interface and holding it to a quality bar that turns research breakthroughs into products people actually build on. That combination is what pulls me in. I've spent my career on data-intensive, technically hard products, taking emerging capability and turning it into something customers trust enough to rely on, and I want to do that for audio AI at the moment it's reshaping how people create and consume content. The accessibility side of the mission matters to me too; making any voice and any language available to anyone is exactly the kind of tangible, universal problem I've always wanted to work on. On the revenue strategy and operations side specifically, I'm drawn to building the pricing, packaging, and go-to-market infrastructure that lets a fast-moving research company scale its commercial engine without slowing the product down.

question: Finally, tell us about a hard problem in your past!
answer: The hardest I've ever worked was rebuilding our customer entitlements system at M Science. I inherited a manual process that ate 40-plus man-hours every week, and automating it meant first doing a brutal amount of manual data validation and transformation to populate the new system. My instinct as an operator is to figure out which work can be simplified or automated and which work simply has to be done. So I cut the scope every way I could, pulling in data science and engineering to find optimizations and personally writing a Python script to handle the complex mapping and shrink the number of records that needed human review. Once I'd exhausted every shortcut, I led a small team through two weeks of focused validation, and I personally reviewed and corrected over 10,000 records to get the data integrity we needed to launch. The automated system replaced the weekly manual grind entirely. It taught me that the unglamorous, detailed work is often exactly what makes a scalable system possible.

I've spent my career as a generalist operator on technically sophisticated products, running discovery, turning insight into strategy, and then building the operational infrastructure to execute it. At M Science I led a cross-functional overhaul of our pricing and packaging that drove a ~35% ARR increase, and I built the master data system that became the semantic layer under our analytics and AI products. Product operations and strategy is where I do my best work, sitting between product, engineering, and go-to-market to make execution repeatable. I'd love to bring that to a company scaling AI in a space where getting it right actually improves people's lives.

question: Asian or Asian American
